input_text: The mean, often called the average, is a way to describe the center of a data set. To find the mean, you add up all the numbers in the set and then divide by how many numbers there are. For example, if we have the numbers 3, 5, and 7, we first find the sum: 3 + 5 + 7 = 15. Since there are 3 numbers, we divide 15 by 3 to get a mean of 5. The formula for the mean is: Mean = (Sum of Values) / (Number of Values). Now, try to find the mean of 10, 20, and 30 on your own. Practice: Calculate the average of {2, 4, 6, 8}. In this lesson, we learned how to calculate the mean to represent a data set.

instructional_path.title: Arithmetic Mean
instructional_path.overview: The mean is a measure of central tendency representing the average value of a data set.
instructional_path.definition: The sum of all values in a set divided by the count of those values.

instructional_path.title: Calculating Mean of Three Numbers
instructional_path.problem_text: Find the mean of the numbers 3, 5, and 7.
instructional_path.solution_summary: Add the numbers to get 15, then divide by the count (3) to find the result 5.

input_text: Perimeter is the total distance around the outside of a shape. For a rectangle, the perimeter is found by adding the lengths of all four sides. Since opposite sides of a rectangle are equal, we can use a shorter method. The formula for the perimeter of a rectangle is: P = 2 × (length + width). Consider a rectangle with a length of 8 cm and a width of 5 cm. To find the perimeter, we calculate 2 × (8 + 5), which is 2 × 13, resulting in 26 cm. Practice: Find the perimeter of a garden that is 10 meters long and 4 meters wide. In this lesson, we explored how to determine the perimeter of rectangular shapes.

instructional_path.title: Perimeter
instructional_path.overview: Perimeter represents the total boundary length of a two-dimensional shape.
instructional_path.definition: The distance around the outside of a shape.

instructional_path.title: Rectangle Perimeter Formula
instructional_path.overview: A specific method to calculate perimeter for rectangles using length and width.
instructional_path.definition: Twice the sum of the length and the width of the rectangle.

instructional_path.title: Perimeter Calculation Example
instructional_path.problem_text: Find the perimeter of a rectangle with length 8 cm and width 5 cm.
instructional_path.solution_summary: Apply the formula by adding 8 and 5, then multiplying the sum by 2.

input_text: Adding fractions is simple when they have the same denominator, which is called a like denominator. To add these fractions, you only add the numerators and keep the denominator the same. For example, to add 1/5 and 2/5, you add 1 + 2 to get 3, so the sum is 3/5. This rule can be written as: a/c + b/c = (a+b)/c. It is important never to add the denominators together. Practice: Solve 3/8 + 4/8 and simplify if possible. In this lesson, we mastered the technique of adding fractions with like denominators.

instructional_path.title: Like Denominators
instructional_path.overview: Like denominators refer to fractions that share the exact same bottom number.
instructional_path.definition: Denominators that are identical in value.

instructional_path.title: Fraction Addition Rule
instructional_path.overview: The procedure for combining fractions that have the same denominator.
instructional_path.definition: Add the numerators together while keeping the denominator unchanged.

instructional_path.title: Adding Basic Fractions
instructional_path.solution_summary: Sum the numerators (1 and 2) to get 3, and place it over the common denominator 5.

input_text: Multiplication is a shortcut for repeated addition of the same number. The numbers being multiplied are called factors, and the result is called the product. For instance, 4 times 3 means adding four three times: 4 + 4 + 4 = 12. We write this as 4 × 3 = 12. An important rule is the Commutative Property, which says that changing the order of factors does not change the product, so 3 × 4 is also 12. Example: If a box has 5 rows of 6 pencils, find the total number of pencils by calculating 5 × 6. Practice: What is the product of 7 and 8? In this lesson, we learned the basic concept and properties of multiplication.

instructional_path.title: Multiplication Basics
instructional_path.overview: Multiplication represents the operation of scaling one number by another or repeated addition.
instructional_path.definition: A mathematical operation that indicates how many times a number is added to itself.

instructional_path.title: Commutative Property
instructional_path.overview: A property stating that the order of factors does not affect the final result.
instructional_path.definition: The rule that a × b = b × a.

instructional_path.title: Pencil Row Calculation
instructional_path.problem_text: A box has 5 rows of 6 pencils. Find the total number of pencils.
instructional_path.solution_summary: Multiply the number of rows (5) by the number of pencils per row (6).

input_text: Elapsed time is the amount of time that passes from the start of an event to the end. To calculate it, you subtract the start time from the end time. If an activity starts at 2:00 PM and ends at 3:30 PM, the elapsed time is 1 hour and 30 minutes. You can also count forward on a clock to find the difference. Example: A movie starts at 7:15 PM and lasts for 2 hours and 10 minutes; it ends at 9:25 PM. Practice: How much time has passed between 10:00 AM and 12:45 PM? In this lesson, we focused on measuring the duration of intervals using elapsed time.

instructional_path.title: Elapsed Time
instructional_path.overview: Elapsed time is the duration or interval between two specific points in time.
instructional_path.definition: The total time that passes from the beginning to the end of an event.

instructional_path.title: Movie Duration Logic
instructional_path.problem_text: A movie starts at 7:15 PM and lasts 2 hours and 10 minutes. When does it end?
instructional_path.solution_summary: Add the duration hours and minutes to the start time to find the completion time.
